seo.title: E-Commerce Voice Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for voice agents AI agents in e-commerce. Covers FTC advertising guidelines, CCPA/GDPR, PCI DSS for payments.

content.intro: Voice agents in e-commerce operate at the intersection of real-time customer interaction and high-stakes transactional data, where a single miscommunication about pricing or return policies can trigger FTC enforcement or mass chargebacks. Unlike text-based interfaces, voice pipelines introduce unique failure modes—latency spikes, speech recognition errors, and TTS distortions—that can corrupt product claims or payment flows before any human review is possible. This checklist ensures your voice agent deployment meets FTC advertising standards, PCI DSS payment security, and ADA accessibility requirements while maintaining the sub-second responsiveness customers expect.

sections.heading: Product Claims & Pricing Accuracy
sections.description: Voice agents must only surface substantiated product claims and real-time accurate pricing to comply with FTC guidelines and avoid refund disputes. Any hallucinated discount, fabricated feature claim, or stale price read aloud constitutes a deceptive trade practice.

items.label: Real-Time Pricing API Integration
items.description: Connect the LLM reasoning layer to a live pricing microservice that fetches current prices, active promotions, and inventory status at query time—never rely on training data or cached responses older than 60 seconds for price quotes.

items.label: Product Claim Substantiation Gate
items.description: Implement a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline that grounds all product feature and benefit claims against your official product catalog and approved marketing copy database before the TTS layer renders the response.

items.label: Superlative and Comparative Claim Filter
items.description: Deploy a post-LLM output classifier that flags and blocks unsubstantiated superlatives ('best,' 'cheapest,' 'guaranteed') and comparative claims ('better than Brand X') unless they are pre-approved and sourced from verified third-party data.

items.label: Price Discrepancy Escalation Protocol
items.description: When the voice agent detects a mismatch between the spoken price and the checkout system price (>$0.01 difference), immediately escalate to a human agent or halt the transaction and inform the customer of the discrepancy before proceeding.

items.label: Promotional Terms Verbalization Requirement
items.description: Configure the agent to always verbalize material promotional conditions (expiration dates, exclusions, minimum purchase thresholds) when quoting a promotional price, mirroring FTC 'clear and conspicuous' disclosure requirements for audio media.

items.label: Catalog Version Pinning and Audit Logging
items.description: Log the exact catalog version, pricing snapshot timestamp, and product data source for every product claim made during a voice session to enable post-hoc audit trails for FTC inquiries or refund dispute resolution.

items.label: Out-of-Stock and Backorder Accuracy Check
items.description: Query inventory availability in real time before confirming product availability to a customer; the agent must never confirm an item is 'in stock and ready to ship' without a live inventory check returning a positive result.

sections.heading: Payment Security & PCI DSS Compliance
sections.description: Voice-based payment collection introduces severe PCI DSS scope expansion risks. Card data spoken aloud must never pass through the LLM layer, and the entire payment sub-flow must be isolated from the conversational AI pipeline.

items.label: LLM Payment Data Exclusion Architecture
items.description: Architect the pipeline so that when a customer enters payment mode, the STT output for card numbers, CVVs, and expiration dates is routed directly to a PCI DSS-certified payment processor (e.g., via DTMF tone capture or a certified voice tokenization service) and never passed to the LLM as input or context.

items.label: DTMF Tone-Based Card Entry Enforcement
items.description: Require customers to enter card numbers via telephone keypad DTMF tones rather than speaking them aloud, eliminating the risk of card data appearing in STT transcripts, LLM context windows, or TTS echo-backs.

items.label: Payment Session Transcript Redaction
items.description: Implement automatic redaction of any payment-related session segment from conversation logs within 30 seconds of session close, replacing card-adjacent utterances with [REDACTED-PCI] tokens before logs are written to any storage system.

items.label: PCI DSS Scope Boundary Documentation
items.description: Produce and maintain a data flow diagram explicitly showing where the voice agent pipeline exits PCI scope and where the certified payment processor takes over, updated with every architecture change and reviewed quarterly.

items.label: Order Confirmation Amount Verbalization
items.description: Before finalizing any purchase, the agent must verbally confirm the exact total charge amount, itemized breakdown, and billing address to the customer and receive explicit verbal confirmation ('yes' or 'confirm') before submitting the transaction.

items.label: Fraud Signal Escalation Trigger
items.description: Integrate with your fraud detection system so that if a transaction is flagged (unusual amount, new device, mismatched billing address), the voice agent immediately pauses the transaction and routes to a human agent rather than proceeding autonomously.

items.label: Payment Failure Handling Script Compliance
items.description: Ensure payment failure responses never reveal specific decline reasons (e.g., 'insufficient funds') that could constitute unauthorized disclosure; use only generic, PCI-approved decline messaging scripts.

sections.heading: Return, Refund & Policy Faithful Representation
sections.description: Voice agents must accurately represent return windows, restocking fees, non-returnable item categories, and refund timelines. Misrepresentation—even unintentional—creates binding verbal commitments and exposes the business to chargeback liability and FTC action.

items.label: Policy Knowledge Base with Version Control
items.description: Maintain a structured, versioned policy knowledge base (return windows, refund timelines, restocking fees, non-returnable SKU categories) that the RAG pipeline queries exclusively for policy questions—never allow the LLM to infer or generalize policy terms from training data.

items.label: Policy Change Propagation SLA
items.description: Establish a maximum 4-hour SLA for propagating any policy change (e.g., holiday return window extension) from the policy management system to the voice agent's knowledge base, with automated validation tests confirming the update before the agent goes live.

items.label: Non-Returnable Item Pre-Purchase Disclosure
items.description: Configure the agent to proactively disclose non-returnable or final-sale status for applicable items before completing a purchase, not only when the customer asks—this mirrors FTC requirements for material terms disclosure.

items.label: Verbal Policy Commitment Recording and Logging
items.description: Flag and log every session in which the agent makes a specific policy commitment (e.g., 'you have 30 days to return this') with a timestamp and policy version reference, creating an auditable record for dispute resolution.

items.label: Ambiguous Policy Query Escalation
items.description: When a customer's policy question falls outside the structured knowledge base (e.g., edge cases, damaged goods, partial returns), the agent must escalate to a human agent rather than generating a speculative answer.

items.label: Refund Timeline Accuracy Enforcement
items.description: Refund processing time estimates spoken by the agent must be pulled from real-time payment processor SLA data, not hardcoded scripts, to account for bank processing variability and avoid creating false expectations.

items.label: Post-Call Policy Summary Delivery
items.description: After any call where a return, refund, or policy commitment was discussed, automatically trigger a follow-up email or SMS to the customer summarizing the exact policy terms verbalized, creating a written record that protects both parties.

sections.heading: Voice Pipeline Safety, Latency & Accessibility
sections.description: The real-time STT→LLM→TTS architecture introduces unique failure modes including speech recognition errors that corrupt intent, latency budget overruns that degrade UX, and accessibility gaps that violate ADA requirements. Each must be systematically addressed.

items.label: End-to-End Latency Budget Enforcement
items.description: Define and enforce a 700ms maximum end-to-end latency budget (STT processing ≤150ms, LLM inference ≤400ms, TTS synthesis ≤150ms) with automated circuit breakers that trigger graceful degradation responses when any component exceeds its budget.

items.label: STT Confidence Threshold with Clarification Loop
items.description: Set a minimum STT confidence threshold of 0.85 for any utterance involving product names, prices, or policy terms; below this threshold, the agent must verbally confirm its interpretation ('Did you say you want to return the blue jacket?') before acting.

items.label: Content Safety Filter in LLM Output Layer
items.description: Deploy a real-time safety classifier between the LLM output and TTS input that blocks responses containing: competitor disparagement, unverified health claims about products, discriminatory language, or any content that could constitute deceptive advertising under FTC guidelines.

items.label: ADA-Compliant Speech Rate and Clarity Controls
items.description: Configure TTS output to default to 140-160 words per minute with clear enunciation, and provide customers with voice commands to request slower speech ('speak slower') or repetition ('repeat that') at any point in the conversation, complying with ADA communication accessibility standards.

items.label: CCPA/GDPR Voice Data Consent Collection
items.description: At the start of every session, play a compliant disclosure informing the customer that the call may be recorded and processed by AI, and capture explicit verbal consent before proceeding—log the consent timestamp and session ID for regulatory audit purposes.

items.label: Conversation Coherence Monitoring with Fallback
items.description: Implement a session-level coherence tracker that detects when the conversation has gone off-rails (topic drift, repeated misunderstandings, customer frustration signals detected via sentiment analysis) and triggers a human handoff offer after two consecutive coherence failures.

items.label: Voice Data Retention and Deletion Policy Enforcement
items.description: Enforce automated deletion of raw voice recordings within 90 days (or per customer deletion request under CCPA/GDPR within 30 days), with separate retention of anonymized transcripts for quality assurance purposes only, stored in a GDPR-compliant jurisdiction.

items.label: Human Escalation Path Always Available
items.description: Ensure the voice agent responds to 'speak to a human,' 'agent,' or 'representative' at any point in the conversation within one turn, without requiring the customer to repeat the request or navigate additional menus—this is both an ADA accessibility requirement and a customer trust baseline.

notes.note: Voice agents making product claims are subject to the same FTC substantiation standards as written advertisements. Any claim about product performance, pricing, or availability spoken by the agent must be substantiated by competent and reliable evidence at the time the claim is made. The FTC's 'clear and conspicuous' disclosure standard applies to audio: material conditions must be verbalized in a manner that is understandable and not buried in rapid speech or post-transaction fine print.

Voice recordings and STT transcripts constitute personal data under both CCPA and GDPR. E-commerce operators must disclose AI voice processing in their privacy policy, obtain consent before recording (with state-specific two-party consent laws applying in CA, FL, IL, and others), honor deletion requests within 30 days (GDPR) or 45 days (CCPA), and maintain a data processing agreement with every third-party STT and TTS vendor in the pipeline.

notes.regulation: PCI DSS v4.0 Scope for Voice Channels
notes.note: PCI DSS v4.0 Requirement 4.2.1 explicitly addresses the protection of cardholder data in voice environments. If any part of the voice pipeline (STT, LLM, TTS, logging) touches card data, it falls within PCI scope and requires full compliance assessment. The recommended architecture is to use DTMF pause-and-resume or a certified voice payment solution that keeps card data entirely outside the AI pipeline, reducing scope to the payment processor only.

notes.regulation: ADA Title III and Section 508 Accessibility
notes.note: E-commerce voice agents serving the public are subject to ADA Title III requirements for effective communication with customers who have disabilities. This includes providing equivalent access for customers with hearing impairments (alternative text-based channels), speech impairments (extended timeout periods, alternative input methods), and cognitive disabilities (simplified language mode, no time pressure on responses). Failure to provide accessible alternatives has resulted in ADA litigation against major e-commerce retailers.

cta.heading: Automate Your E-Commerce Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your voice agent pipeline against this checklist—automatically flagging pricing inaccuracies, policy misrepresentations, and PCI scope violations before they reach your customers.
